segments.title: The T.3 Velos and its Greek Navy mission
segments.summary: The T.3 Velos was a two-seat Blackburn torpedo aircraft built for the Greek Navy's coastal-defence, reconnaissance, bombing, and training duties.
segments.body: The Velos developed the two-seat Blackburn Dart concept into the T.3, the third aircraft in the firm's torpedo-aircraft series. Major F. A. Bumpus designed it in 1925 around a 450-horsepower Napier Lion V engine for a Greek Navy requirement. Its coastal-defence brief combined torpedo delivery with reconnaissance, bombing, and training, making one airframe serve several fleet-air-arm duties. The Velos was primarily a seaplane that could receive a wheeled undercarriage when required. That arrangement reversed the Dart's concept, which began as a deck-landing aircraft that could be converted into a seaplane.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies the Velos as a two-seat Dart development for a Greek Navy coastal-defence role and contrasts its seaplane configuration with the Dart.

segments.title: Why rugged reliability mattered more than a sleek airframe
segments.summary: A comparison with a smoother Blackburn design explains why the proven Dart's rugged reliability remained valuable in single-engine naval aviation.
segments.body: The video contrasts the Velos and Dart family with a visibly smoother, more aerodynamic Blackburn aircraft, raising the question of why the older-looking design persisted. Cost was part of the answer, but the central argument is that the Dart had already proved exceptionally reliable. Dr. Clarke presents rugged reliability as the quality customers particularly associated with Blackburn aircraft. For single-engine naval aviation, that established dependability could take priority over the attraction of a more refined shape.

evidence.note: The presenter contrasts aircraft forms, then says cost and the Dart's established reliability explain Blackburn's appeal, especially for single-engine naval aircraft.

segments.title: Greek production and flexible Velos crew arrangements
segments.summary: Blackburn's Greek production work supported training-capable Velos aircraft while retaining a single-seat torpedo and coastal-defence role.
segments.body: During the second half of 1925, Blackburn took over and completed the Greek National Aircraft Factory before receiving a five-year contract to operate it with local labour. Two demonstration aircraft were built with identical cockpits and dual controls for shipment to Athens so crews could train before the first Greek-built machines arrived. Operational Velos aircraft carried coastal-defence equipment, including a Lewis gun on a flush-mounted Scarff ring behind the pilot. The rear cockpit had dummy controls and was occupied for bombing, reconnaissance, or instructional sorties. When a torpedo was carried, however, the Velos flew as a single-seater, showing how crew arrangement changed with the mission.

evidence.note: The transcript describes Blackburn's Greek factory contract, dual-control training machines, coastal-defence equipment, and single-seat torpedo operation.
